discussion_comments.comment_body:
This check doesn't guarantee that objects `a` and `b` are equal. For example:
```ts
isEqual({a: 1, b: undefined}, {a: 1, c: 1}) //=> true
```

I didn't saw that possibility...
I added a check for existance of the property in b object with hasOwnProperty in a new commit. @webNeat Do you see other cases where this check would not be enough to check equality between a and b objects ? or can I resolve this ?

The only other case I can see with normal values is the case of `NaN`
```
const a = NaN
console.log(a === a) //=> false
```

I think we can leave the case of NaN Like it is, for objects with NaN values in it will return false but as NaN === NaN returns false too this behavior is more consistant than returning true (I think returning a different result than the === operator for non object values would be inconsistant with the goal that function which is to provider an === operator with a consistant behavior of === check on values of objects instead of their memory adress).

So if you don't have any more problematic cases I mark this thread as resolved.

I understand your point, if the goal is to provide an operator that behaves exactly the same as `===` then I agree with you.
if in the other hand, the goal is to use this to remove duplicated values, then two instances of `NaN` should be considered the same value, this is what the native `Set` does:
```ts
new Set([NaN, 1, NaN]) //=> Set(2) { NaN, 1 }
```
I have also checked the implementation of `eq` method from lodash and it seems to use the same logic of the `Set`: https://github.com/lodash/lodash/blob/master/test/eq.test.js#L9
```ts
_.eq(NaN, NaN) //=> true
```
What do you think?

I'm not sure of that, the symbolic of NaN is to express a non numeric value that you tried to use as a number. But this is used no matter what non numeric value you have then it make sense that NaN != NaN as if you try to test 2 * x === 3 * x except for x = 0 you should always have false. If NaN === NaN you would here have 2 * x === 3 * x returning true for x = 'a' for exemple. So for me it makes sense returning false on isEqual(NaN, NaN) even it makes the array having multiple NaN. 

But I also understand your point of vue and honestly I can't say which one is the best point of vue.
Maybe @colinhacks as the owner of Zod could choose which behavior makes the more sense for him ?

I would add that for me this point is not really a crucial concern has using Zod  with typescript strict type checking, you should never see your code generate NaN values and so this choice should quite never have any consequence.
Let's revert this to using a native `Map` here which has good support (IE11+).
Do not use Set for intersection as it is not supported in legacy mobile browsers (like Android 4.4.4).
